user.name: Yick Ho Pang
user.description: Co-founder @ DivineSide | AI systems for tutoring/test prep centers | Referrals, reviews, follow-up on autopilot | 19, building in public

tweets.text:
This post exists because of a spreadsheet.

I tracked every post I've written, saw which topic and format were converting, and wrote today's post to match. Using the data of past posts to tell me what to write.

I'm 19, building this all in public.
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
If you're posting on LinkedIn/X for business and not tracking which posts convert, you're posting randomly and hoping.

Hope isn't a content strategy. Log the hook, the format, the topic, and the numbers, then write more of what already worked.

What do you track?
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
Once a day my scraper scrapes 4 metrics per post: views, likes, comments, reposts.

My dashboard then shows my top 5 posts by engagement with their hooks, median engagement by type and topic, and my real content mix vs target.

Then I write more of what's working.
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
Every post I publish gets 9 fields logged at posting time into a csv:

post_type, framework, topic, format, the differentiator it draws, the hook word-for-word, the closing, platform, funnel stage.

Then I'll used a scraper to fill in the metrics to help me write my next post.
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
My content rule runs on a giant csv with metrics of past posts.

A hook that hit 2,000 impressions last month? I run it again with a new angle. A format that's averaged 0 engagement across 15 posts? I kill it.

That's it. I just double down on what's working.
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
I built a tracker so I stop guessing what to post.

Every post gets logged with its hook, format, and topic. Once a week I add the engagement numbers. Then I just write more of what's already converting.

How do you guys decide what to post?
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
The real money is in the games measured in years, not months.

Longer games have fewer players, so that's where the least competition and the biggest payoff sit. Fish where the fewest go.

Are you planning for this quarter, or this decade?
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
A compelling offer beats a convincing argument.

Stop making the customer carry all the risk. Take it on yourself, define the result clearly, make it a no-brainer.

My test: if your offer doesn't scare you a little, it isn't strong enough. Who carries the risk in yours?
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
Everyone wants the advanced tactic. The secret funnel, the new ad targeting, the clever hack.

The biggest businesses just do the fundamentals exceptionally well. The people selling you advanced tactics usually don't have a big business themselves.

Do the basics.
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang

tweets.text:
Marketing's real job is to make selling redundant.

Build so much desire that people show up already sold. Walk into an Apple store, nobody hard-sells you a MacBook, the staff just take orders. They earned that.

If you're still hard-selling, you haven't built enough desire.
tweets.name: Yick Ho Pang
